data.title: Vista Gold: The Market Sees A Transformative Future In An Economically Robust Mt Todd
data.description:
A “Buy” Rating for Vista Gold Corp.

Vista Gold Corp. (VGZ), a company based in Englewood, Colorado, which is developing future gold production in Australia, is a Buy in our opinion, consistent with our previous article from May 29, 2025, and with the general market sentiment.

In the “About Vista Gold Corp.” section of Vista Gold's third quarter 2025 financial results (“Q3 2025 Results”) reported by Seeking Alpha, the company describes itself as follows:

Vista holds the Mt Todd gold project, a leading development-stage gold deposit located in the Tier-1 mining jurisdiction of Northern Territory, Australia. The Project offers strong project economics, significant initial production, expansion potential, development optionality, exploration upside, advanced local infrastructure, and broad community support.

Our Update

Since our last Buy rating, shares of Vista Gold Corp. (VGZ), hereinafter "VGZ", listed on the NYSE, have risen by 60.66%, visibly outperforming the US stock market index. At the time of this article, the S&P 500 had gained only 14.97% over the same period. The difference could be even greater, as the S&P 500 only includes the largest publicly traded companies in the US, and its performance in recent years has been heavily influenced by the hype surrounding massive investments in AI.

It wasn't the seductive call of a siren that propelled the stock from its previous level to dizzying heights, but rather a turning point in a transformative future for VGZ Company. Fueled by the desire to solve the puzzle, demand focused on reconciling VGZ Company's decision to scale down the Mount Todd gold project with Mount Todd's projected potential to generate outstanding returns, sending the share price into a meteoric rise. The ability to trigger a price surge is extremely ambitious and still holds considerable potential. With the likely cause of this success now on the verge of being unravelled, it's time to provide an update on our Buy rating.

From Our Previous Article

In our previous article, we expressed our view that a Buy rating was appropriate for investors interested in Vista Gold Corp. via its NYSE-listed shares. We were convinced that these shares would benefit from rising demand, as the Mount Todd gold project was due for a revaluation, with investors considering the increased likelihood of Mt Todd developing into a producing mine with a reduced scale and greater adaptability to the current global economic climate. This was not just any gold mining project, but a highly profitable one with remarkable exploration potential in Australia's mining-friendly Northern Territory. The project was well-secured in terms of infrastructure and local community support. Given the rising gold price, VGZ was therefore ideally positioned for tremendous growth.

Feasibility Study for the Scaled-Down Mt Todd Gold Project

The Mt Todd project – wholly owned by Vista Gold Corp. – is located 40 km northwest of Katherine in the Northern Territory, Australia, and is considered by the company to be one of Australia’s largest gold deposits under development. Gold extraction will be carried out via open-pit mining. According to the company’s revised feasibility study, the project remains economically viable with a capacity of 15,000 tonnes per day (the “2025 FS”). This profitability will have a positive impact on the company over the entire life of the mine, assuming gold prices remain robust. The area also offers very good expansion potential, which should reflect solidity and suggest growth. In order to commence construction of the Mt Todd open-pit gold mine, the various necessary operating and environmental permits are in place. These permits are subject to change due to regulatory requirements arising from the resizing of the project, but VGZ sounds convincing in suggesting that it will not take longer than 18 months (from July 2025) to have things settled with the permits amended as well.

The left side of the slide shows the Mt. Todd gold project before the revision, while the remainder depicts its transformation into a 15,000-tonne-per-day per day capacity feasibility study (5.3 million tonnes per annum).

From our perspective, the scaled-down feasibility study shows that VGZ intends to substantiate its plan to establish gold production in Australia's Northern Territory with facts, and that this will not just be a series of long-standing purposes, unfulfilled promises and enticing figures on slides. With the price of gold continuing its unabated climb to historic highs, VGZ is pursuing accelerated value realization. A revised feasibility study, projecting a production rate of 15,000 tonnes per day, requiring minimal financing and reducing initial investment costs by ≈ (“approximately”) 60%, significantly increases the likelihood of developing the Mt. Todd gold mine.

Frederick H. Earnest, President and CEO of Vista Gold Corp., said (from “2025 FS”):

This Study marks a significant shift in the strategy for Mt Todd, […] incorporating methods to reduce development and operational risks.

As the feasibility study “2025 FS” illustrates, these methods primarily aim to promote “Strong and Stable Gold Production from Well-Defined Deposit ”, according to the company.

The graphic from the company's “2025 FS” illustrates annual gold production over 15 years. After optimising the processing plant in years 2 through 4, stable production volumes are expected from years 5 through 15, preceded by a ramp-up in the first year. With an AISC LOM of US$1,499 per ounce, the company joins the ranks of global competitors, many of whom are stunned by the blows of a high AISC that, with an average of $1,544/oz as the benchmark for all, is already enormously higher than Mt Todd.

And there's more: While the global industry's AISC/oz has risen steadily over time and may continue to rise, Vista Gold promises in its “2025 FS” document that the AISC/oz for the Mt Todd project will be around $1,499/oz for the first 15 years of the project's life and then for a total project life of 30 years.

The Exploration Potential

Given the enormous exploration potential of the surrounding resources and the remaining untapped resources, value creation is expected to continue for a long time, as the gold price, as it currently stands, remains very promising.

The mine shoreline could at any time flood the 35-year production (146,000 oz/year over the entire mining period / proven and probable reserves – 5.2 million oz averaging 0.94 g/t), with an additional 1.8 to 3.5 million ounces of gold in the Measured, Indicated and Inferred Mineral Resource base averaging ≈0.8145 g/t from the 24 km Batman-Driffield trend and 496,000 ounces of gold averaging ≈1.15 g/t from the Quigleys deposit in the Measured and Inferred Mineral Resources. The success of the Batman deposit could extend to the northeast, where equally valuable deposits exist in all directions. This would allow the exploration team to drill and conduct follow-up studies in those areas. It should be noted that upgrading these higher-grade resources to the upper category will improve the quality of future reserves, which should have a positive impact on the business, as reserves will lead to higher returns through improved operational sustainability and better economics. This gives Mt Todd the opportunity to strengthen its cohort in the highly competitive global gold mining market, and shares of VGZ stock on the NYSE will gain extra points.

In addition, the enclave, with mining permits and resources that can be upgraded through exploration, is located within at least 1,337 km² of adjacent licenses to explore for precious and base metal discoveries. These district-level exploration areas could reserve a swarm of impetus for the market value of VGZ shares to continue soaring in the future.

VGZ Vista Gold Corp.'s Market Value

At the time of this writing, shares of Vista Gold Corp., which trade on the NYSE under the symbol “VGZ”, were trading at $1.99 per share, representing a market capitalisation of $247.37 million.

Technical Analysis

The share price is 35.8% above the average of $1.465 per share within its 52-week range of $0.47 to $2.46 per share. The 20-day moving average (20-MA) fell below the 50-day moving average (50-MA) at the beginning of last month, which, following the correction in the gold price from its all-time high in mid-October and the subsequent consolidation, indicated a bearish sentiment. This temporary downward momentum has now weakened, as the 20-MA, driven by the gold price action (last month +5.24% in the Gold Spot Price (XAUUSD:CUR)). It should not be long before the price breaks through the 50-day moving average, confirming a solid uptrend in the short term as well, in addition to the longer-term of the MA-200 Cross. Although the RSI at 61.41x is closer to overbought levels than before, everything suggests that the market cap can still impress. Based on the recent correlation between the stock price recovery and the RSI, the market capitalisation even managed to scale up by 150% (from late August to mid-September), despite the narrow range signalled by the RSI. But we must acknowledge the tailwind from the announcement of the “2025 FS” that we referred to earlier in this analysis. We are confident that providing financing for the construction of the Mt. Todd mine will give substance to this project, pave the way for its transformation, and inject new momentum into the share price.

Valuation

The “2025 FS” claims that Mt Todd has “robust economics”, namely the following:

Although this may not apply to all price ranges, the most likely method for estimating the net present value (NPV) is to assume a linear relationship between the gold price and the after-tax net present value at 5% discount rate. The calculated estimates for the net present value are:

A) ≈ $3.43 billion at the current gold price of $4,196.05 per ounce.

B) ≈ $3.78 billion based on Deutsche Bank's 2026 forecast of $4,450 per ounce.

C) ≈ $4.54 billion based on Deutsche Bank's 2026 forecast of prices around $5,000 per ounce.

Vista Gold has a number of shares outstanding of 126.21 million (see Seeking Alpha here under “ Trading Data”), and if, which is never the case as the company uses as much venture capital as debt capital, they raise $425 million for the construction of the Mt Todd gold mine through the issuance of additional ordinary shares on the stock market, the current volume of shares outstanding would increase to a total of 339.7 million pieces based on the current share price on the NYSE. The calculations for the individual scenarios are as follows:

A) The net present value (NPV) per share is $10.10 at the current gold price.

B) The NPV per share (based on Deutsche Bank's forecast for 2026: $4,450/ounce) is $11.13.

C) The NPV per share (based on Deutsche Bank's forecast for 2026: gold price is around $5,000/ounce) is $13.40.

Vista Gold owns 100% of the Mt Todd project. This means that even in the (unlikely) event of raising all the capital for construction through a stock offering, the potential value of these shares on the NYSE could be 407% to 575% higher than the current share price of $1.99.

Also, Mt Todd's "2025 FS" projects after-tax free cash flow of $1.6 billion for the first 15 years of commercial operation, based on a gold price of $2,500. Mt Todd's average annual after-tax free cash flow is $106.67 million. This puts its market capitalisation-to-after-tax free cash flow ratio at 2.32x, compared to the sector median of 9.98x. Given current gold prices and Deutsche Bank's gold price forecasts, Mt Todd's future cash flow, which is an “after-tax free” measure of cash flow, could be even higher, and to an even more favourable comparison with the sector median.

The Risk Section

VGZ has liquid assets of $13.72 million and is debt-free. Currently, its ongoing business operations use approximately $2 million in liquid assets per quarter. Assuming the company does not raise capital through a share issue – as it has done so far – these liquid assets will last for a maximum of two years.

The company will finance the construction of the mine with debt in addition to equity, which is the most likely scenario. The debt burden on the balance sheet has implications for the sentiment around Vista Gold shares on the NYSE, as the company will then have to divert funds otherwise earmarked for further growth to repay the debt and pay interest, as has been the custom in the industry worldwide since its inception. But it is also true that lenders' lending at a time when credit conditions generally remain tight will further improve sentiment around VGZ shares. Western economies in general, including the Northern Territory Province in Australia, are less credible today than they were before COVID-19. As a sign of the strained economic situation, the Reserve Bank of Australia left its key interest rate unchanged at 3.6% at its November 2025 meeting to combat excessive inflation, which peaked in 2022. Interest rates are still at their highest in over a decade, and policymakers highlighted the continuing uncertainty surrounding the national and global outlook (based on Trading Economics as of this article). In contrast to this scenario, which is not at all conducive to credit provision, as it was before Covid-19 in 2020 and all the other crises that have followed since then, including the energy crisis, wars and inflation, it implies that lenders will see Mt Todd as a valuable asset both now and in the future if VGZ is successful in obtaining loans. The likelihood of this happening is certainly higher now than in the pre-2025 FS period, as technical consultants with proven experience in Australian and international projects have re-sized Vista Gold Corp.'s Mt Todd gold mine project into one that is easier to implement. Assuming they are rational in their view, stock market forces can only be very pleased with this, at least if lenders, despite their cautious stance on a weaker economy, see the value in Mt. Todd to handing over monetary resources to Vista Gold Corp. And perhaps it will become one of the most sought-after stocks if value versus growth wins the tug-of-war amid fears of a potential AI bubble. Just imagine what this unprecedented combination could mean for VGZ stock: a fantastic gold price and investors flocking to value stocks.

Mt Todd's Independent Technical Consultants Mitigate Risk

The Mt Todd report “2025 FS” lists some independent technical consultants (“experts”) commissioned by Vista Gold to prepare the study.

Below are some of the most important gold mining projects that the same consultants have been working on recently and that are already in operation.

GRES has engineered and commissioned significant gold projects:

  • The processing facilities at the Mt. Ida gold/silver open-pit mine, owned by Aurenne Group Mining, were completed ahead of schedule in April 2023. According to online information, the Mt. Ida gold project commenced gold production in May 2023 and is considered the company's flagship project.
  • The 2.4 million tonne per year gold processing plant and associated infrastructure at the Warrawoona mine, owned by West Coast Gold Pty Ltd. The plant was commissioned in 2022 as an open-pit mine with a standalone CIL processing plant. Since July 5, 2024, the plant has been undergoing care and maintenance. The Warrawoona mine received credit concessions to finance operations and further development over time.
  • GRES carried out modifications to the existing Sunrise Dam process plant to increase gold recovery at AngloGold Ashanti's (AU) Sunrise Dam Mine, located 50 km south of Laverton, Western Australia. This is an active open-pit and underground gold producing 259,000 ounces in 2024 at an AISC of US$1,665/oz.
  • GRES upgraded the existing processing plant at Carosue Dam, operated by Northern Star Resources Limited (OTCPK:NESRF) (OTCPK:NSTYY), located 106 km northeast of Kalgoorlie-Boulder, Western Australia. The assets include active gold mining in both open-pit and underground mines. 240 koz for F25 gold production on 4,198 koz mineral reserves as of March 31, 2025, and 917 koz ore reserves as of March 31, 2025.

As for Mining Plus, this company engages in drilling operations at the Costerfield Gold Mine, owned by Alkane Resources Ltd (OTCPK:ALKEF) (ALK:CA). The Costerfield Gold Mine is a high-grade underground gold and antimony mine located 10 km east of Heathcote, Victoria, Australia. According to the Alkane Resources Ltd website, in 2024, the mine produced 43,346 ounces of gold (Au) and 1,282 tonnes of antimony (Sb), leading to 54,805 ounces of equivalent gold. Alkane Resources Ltd states (on its website):

At 31 December 2024, Mineral Resources and Ore Reserves for Costerfield stood at:

Total Mineral Resources (Measured and Indicated): 1.197Mt at 8.3g/t gold (320koz) and 2.5% antimony (30.2kt) Total Mineral Resources (Inferred): 538kt at 7.5g/t gold (130koz) and 1.8% antimony (9.7kt) Total Ore Reserves (Proven + Probable): 604kt at 8.7g/t gold (168koz) and 1.8% antimony (11.1kt)

The Gold Price

The outlook for gold prices is too positive to fear a halt to this rally and a sharp decline: Gold Spot Price (XAUUSD:CUR) is at $4,196.05/ounce at the time of writing, up 60.5% year-to-date, driven by robust demand for safe-haven assets and hopes for interest rate cuts from the Federal Reserve. Leading analysts expect further increases in gold prices: Deutsche Bank’s 2026 forecast has been mentioned earlier in this analysis. Seeking Alpha previously reported that: UBS raised its bullish estimate to $4,900 per ounce (up from $4,700 per ounce). The mid-2026 price target, according to UBS, is $4,500 per ounce (up from $4,200 per ounce). The bearish estimate of $3,700 per ounce remains unchanged.

Average Volume (3 Months)

With 1.85 million VGZ shares traded on the NYSE, this stock is not very liquid, so having to sell shares to cash in can lead to unpleasant consequences, such as poor performance, delays, or even losses. This is because the sale price may have to be lowered so that the market, where not many VGZ shares are traded daily, can absorb the demand and buy back the shares.

Conclusion

Vista Gold Corp., a NYSE-listed company headquartered in Englewood, Colorado, USA, is developing the Mt. Todd gold mine in Australia. In our view, the stock is a buy.

The company now plans to build a smaller-scale gold production facility in Australia. Vista Gold has revised its project compared to the original version, reducing initial capital expenditures by nearly 60%. The location offers favourable exploration and mining regulations, enjoys strong local support, and has solid infrastructure. We believe the project now has a better chance of securing financing, as this factor has not yet been addressed for the operations and construction to begin. Should Vista Gold be able to convince investors and, at a later stage, lenders to secure the necessary financing, this would significantly underscore Vista Gold Corp.'s position as a valuable and reliable company, amid a context in which credit conditions remain constrained by global economic uncertainty, and investors are much less inclined than in the past to expose their wealth to the risk in the financial markets.

The experts who advised on the revised Mt. Todd project have demonstrable experience with similar Australian gold mine projects. This aspect, together with the project's robust economic metrics, is no guarantee of success, but the prospects for success are very promising.

data.title: My 2026 Market Outlook Is Negative For Growth, Neutral/Positive For Value
data.description:
In most of my articles for the past 2 months, I have been unequivocally conservative on near-term or medium-term market movements. I have, in fact, moved to a portion of cash of my total portfolio (over 40% at the time of writing), which is something I never thought I would be doing again in my investment career.

That was, of course, before the AI craze and what I (and others) view as a bubble.

However, if you follow my work, you'll know that I tend to view movements or trends such as these as challenges - and quite "fun" ones. I was in the front seat of several investment communities and chats while the COVID-19 drop was ongoing.

I was chatting, I was buying, I was viewing how investors, both young and old, new and experienced, were reacting. It was my first real crash, because I hadn't started investing seriously in -08. My own mindset at the time surprised me, truth be told, because I was much calmer than expected. As the market was dropping over 14% in a single day, I was carefully selecting my buys. I didn't sell anything. I just bought and held. I allocated hundreds of thousands of dollars over a short time, with conviction.

And it paid off. Over the past 4-5 years, I have more than doubled my total portfolio value. A "doubling" in 4-5 years is superb, compared to the typical average or typical usual measure, the rule of 72.

Going forward, as an investor, I'm obviously looking for a repeat performance of this performance. I want to double my portfolio value again, and I want to do it in as similar a way as I can.

However, some of my investments at the time were fairly broad-based. I do not believe that outperforming the market will be possible using those strategies.

Why don't I believe that, and what do I see for the market?

That's what I'm going to be looking at here.

The market in 2026 - I do not see good potential.

A few things to begin with.

No one can predict with 100% accuracy where the market is going to go. Not me, not a computer, not anyone.

The statements I'm going to make here are my views based on the facts and metrics available to me. They are, in essence, my "best guesses". But that's also what any analyst's texts or predictions are. Best guesses.

Remember also to tailor your investments to your individual needs. Someone who's going to be buying a property in 2026 should be considering investing differently than someone who's a homeowner and retired. How you should invest, in my view, depends on where you are in life. There's no "one size fits all"-approach in this game. At this stage, me and the clients I have are mature investors. That may not be you.

As a value investor, I look at a number of indicators for where the market is, not just at the S&P 500, even if this is the topic of this article. Some of the more common ones I look at are the average P/E ratio on a basis for 10 years, with the TTM 10-year EPS growth as a divisor for the calculation. This essentially allows me to see how, relative to history, over- or undervalued a market is. Putting that into a historical context then allows me to make an educated interpretation on where that is, and based on that, I try to make a prediction of where things are likely to go.

So, what facts do we know here? And facts are, of course, indisputable data known to be true.

In calculating the SPY (or European index numbers), I adjust the cyclical P/E to their geometric, rather than their arithmetic means, making it better for rates of change. Valuation depends upon the lens you use to view the market, and you can view the market (SPY) in this context as being overvalued from anywhere to almost 200%, to as little as 119%. The calculations I use for the Swedish and German markets are expressed very well in the average of four valuation indicators found here, putting the SPY at 155% overvaluation on average.

European markets are slightly different, but all are overvalued as well.

Again, based on those four indicators.

What does this tell us?

It tells us that by using these approaches and averaging them, we get the implication that the current level of valuation for the SPY has rarely been seen in history before 2020. As the authors write, 3 standard deviations above the historical mean. I would say with conviction that these indicators aren't useful necessarily as short-term implicatures for what might happen, but I don't invest short term. I don't care if the market short-term drops or rises; what I'm worried about is what the medium to longer-term implications tell me.

The overvaluation of the SPY (and other markets)

As I said, there are multiple ways to view this. Some would say that on a forward P/E ratio, the market is a bit more than one standard deviation above the norm.

I want to avoid this becoming an article centered on mathematical means to determine value and overvaluation, so let me just say that we know that, based on historical data, most indicators show that the SPY is between 1-3 standard deviations above its historical average.

Some would argue that the Dot-com bubble was higher - some would not. But that we are significantly above the average based on P/E indicators (but not necessarily others), that is indisputable. So now we know that.

Does this matter? Obviously, I think it does.

I think any bubble not based on actual asset value, meaning fundamentals, is based on a combination of speculative excitement, of herd mentality, of FOMO (Fear of missing out), of the famous theory that prices will keep rising ad infinitum (the greater fool theory). This, back in the dot-com bubble and other bubbles, led to a combination of significant overvaluation, capital misallocation on a very high order.

All of this, in my view, can lead to only one eventual outcome: Decline.

The question is only when.

Where you stand here depends essentially on where you stand in the bubble argument. So let me (briefly) describe those.

1. The current bubble argument is exaggerated - AI provides innovation and value that justifies it

If you are in this "camp," your stance is that the current market is a justified reflection of the innovation, but current and potential, that AI and technology are going to bring about. Arguments of this camp are centered around, apart from innovation, net issuance % of market cap, which do not indicate a bubble at this time, even though these implications point to a net issuance bubble in say, Bitcoin.

If you hold this view, you can argue that you're in good company, with heavyweights like Goldman Sachs Research not seeing a bubble either, and also also differences from past bubbles.

Many of these will admit that there are signs of excessive speculation in the market, but the fundamental view that we're in an "AI bubble" does not hold water, including net stock buybacks, etc., and rising, but not yet peak IPO valuations.

It should be noted that even Goldman Sachs argues for diversification and a reduced reliance on tech, which is them arguing against themselves.

This point is me "arguing against myself," which I believe to be a crucial point of any investment. Find the opposite argument, and if you're unable to "argue against yourself," then you're on thin ice with your thesis.

If you're in this camp, you do not believe we're in a bubble, or you believe the valuations have far higher to go, so it's not worth considering the market overvaluation.

2. Nothing in this trend is justified by fundamentals - get out, and get out fast

This takes the opposite view of the market. The value of the current tech companies outpaces any forward expectation of cash flows; there is intense speculation about the profitability, applicability, and usability of AI, and the spending levels are outpacing current profits.

There is currently a confirmed divergence between spending and CapEx/Revenue, indicating that companies are overspending on infrastructure, without there being actual proof that this will generate revenue on par with those investments. Things like there needing $2T worth of combined revenues to fund the supposed/expected computing power. The tech sector itself has warned of a spending bubble, a frenzy. Analysts have for some time said that the returns for these investments, the payoffs are uncertain.

Traditional software companies are bleeding cash and value. We're talking about investments of over $400B per year with supposed convictions. Productivity gains? Uncertain, or "nascent" at best, even Pro-AI arguments acknowledge this about the current trends.

New revenue streams? Mostly absent. We're mostly talking about ways to make the human parts of processors/processing obsolete, or being more efficient. This is good, but how does that differ from earlier tools, or justify that this tool should be worth as much?

By the end of this year, the largest tech companies will have spent almost $800 million worth of CapEx in 3 years. Over that time, they have never had to show revenue growth at the rate that that level of investment in any other company or sector would require.

Let me clarify that I don't mean that these companies aren't growing revenues - they aren't growing revenues from AI investments in that size. Back in 2024, Microsoft said that it was making less than $850M from AI-based revenue sources. This grew to above a billion at the beginning of the year (2025). They haven't reported AI-specific growth or revenues since.

Even if we assume that AI revenues have doubled, that comes to $2B out of an LTM MSFT revenue of $245, less than 1% for a sector where the company is investing close to over 50-80% of its annual revenues over a period of 3 years. These costs are also being adjusted on the other side - such as OpenAI (OPENAI) spending on computing is done "heavily discounted," so MSFT is basically only paying what it costs to operate the servers. It seems to me that the entire sector is "waiting for gains and growth," which I believe is unlikely to exist in the first place.

I don't want to give you the impression that I spend my days finding arguments against an AI bubble. I have been peripherally keeping up with what is going on, because signs of overvaluation have been clear to me for some time.

To summarize this view, I would say that the facts are that companies aren't seeing returns on AI yet. This is a fact, as confirmed by this and this, some up to 96%.

The counter-argument is that those returns are coming - I do not believe they will come to the degree that justifies the current valuation.

Thus, my stance is that the market will decline or crash.

The resulting view

I hope this gives you a basic insight into the two perspectives that (I see) are currently driving trends in the market.

You may come down in the middle of these perspectives, or be on either end.

What worries me personally the most is the historical and simple data, unrelated to any one sector, even tech. The thing that "just" looks at the SPY and considers that based on these and these metrics (see above), we are now at a historical valuation where, based on points in history that were characterized by bubbles, we're going to see potentially a decade of negative or weak returns. I'm talking about 1965, I'm talking about 1929, and I'm talking about 2000.

The question I often have is what sort of results some of the larger, high-value companies need to present before the market grows "skittish" with their valuation. This is a question, again, no one can answer with 100% accuracy.

The facts are already on the table. The returns yielded from AI investments are (currently) not justifying the capital expenditures. The markets, the companies, and analysts are saying "wait and see".

The market and analysts are currently accepting this - for now - but we'll see how that looks in the future.

My view is that when that day comes in earnest, we'll see a brief and sudden exodus from the market, similar to COVID-19 or the dotcom bubble, likely less than a month. Given today's focus on Algo trading, index trading, and fast trading, this movement will be massive, it will be irrational (as I believe today's market valuation is), and very few sectors will be entirely spared. Country markets will not be spared either. Sweden, Germany, France, they'll go down too - maybe more than the U.S.

We very recently had a major financial institution issuing guidance that was closest to my own guidance - BoA.

This guidance sees at most a 4% upside from current levels. My own forecast is between negative 5% to positive 5%, averaging at 0% growth. I am therefore forecasting that returns will be sub-par and poor in general, but I am not "forecasting a bubble popping."

I think due to the underlying investment fundamentals, it will take longer. Note that the people who have gone against the grain, or forecasted crashes, have always been a minority.

Also, they are never right.

Until they are - and then they become "legends", like Jeremy Grantham.

What am I doing

The reason I'm forecasting an anemic rate of return for the SPY is that I believe that there will at least be some acknowledgement of a bubble in AI spending. I believe it will express itself in slowing growth, potentially a downturn, or volatility. In slowing investments. In slowing earnings growth.

This means I am:

  1. Rotating or selling off any investment that has reached its target, taking profits where possible (and justified). You can see this in my continued article coverage.
  2. Maintain a much larger cash position than normally (over 40% at this time).
  3. Shorten my investment cycles - 20-35% RoR is now enough for me to rotate, as opposed to 50-90% 2 years ago.
  4. Being extremely careful about what I invest in, and also with smaller positions when I invest.
  5. Focusing on companies that, even though they will take hits in a crash, are generally uncorrelated to broader AI and tech.
  6. Shoring up my cash in generative and "printing" investments with zero or close-to-zero risks, such as interest accounts or alternative asset investments/classes. (3-19% yield/RoR for me, outside of the market).

Note that I am also advising others with their investments in my role as an advisor in a professional capacity. I'm able to tell you that what I am doing for myself, I also have them doing/doing for them. This is not a lightly reached conclusion or movement (nor is it advice to you, the reader) - it represents significant capital movements given the AUM/capital involved both for me and for others.

It's fair to call myself and the clients I have "mature" investors. They are not early-stage investors with a high tolerance for loss. Nor am I - I cater to individuals and companies with different mindsets. The focus on capital preservation and safety, accepting in return lower rates of return during some periods, is characteristic here.

Which is why I speak about needing to tailor your approach for you, and no one else.

My S&P500 prediction

I predict an S&P 500 2026E end-of-year target of 6,800, about the same level as we're seeing today, for the reasons mentioned above. I take a bearish stance. I do not believe that a bubble pop may be imminent, but the danger of it still leads me to shore up my defenses and shift how I view the market for about 6 months now.

It represents a view where capital protection and a 5-10% RoR for 2026E is acceptable (even if I look for 15%+ annualized when I invest for the longer term), in exchange for not being exposed to a potential 15-30% crash if and when that crash does come.

It represents capital preservation above exuberance.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: Netflix: Sell After The Warner Bros. Discovery Acquisition
data.description:
I initiate coverage on Netflix, Inc. (NFLX) after Q3 results proved that the growth trajectory remains intact, despite the one-time $619M Brazilian tax expense that dragged operating margin to 28.2% versus prior 31.5% guidance.

In the near term, I am not bullish on Netflix. In fact, I rate this stock as a Sell after the company announced the acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery, Inc. (WBD) in a $72B deal.

Aside from the complexity of this transaction, with WBD now required to divest its cable networks business before the deal closes in Q3 2026, there are regulatory concerns. Given that Netflix and HBO Max would combine two of the biggest subscription services in the US, this could lead to less competition in paid streaming. From what I'm reading, Netflix has potentially offered a $5B breakup fee if regulators block the deal. The official PR didn't mention any details of this $5B breakup fee, which adds to the risks. Speaking of risks, Paramount Skydance (PSKY) has questioned the deal, stating that WBD favored a Netflix bid over several previous offers from Paramount (which included the cable network business).

In this article, I will show that price action suggests that investors are not excited about this deal. There are many reasons, although funding is the key one for me. At $27.75 per share, the acquisition value for WBD implies $72B in equity value ($82.7B in EV). Considering the liquidity of the company (and the fact that each WBD shareholder will obtain $23.25 in cash and $4.501 in shares of Netflix common stock for each share of WBD), I believe debt will spike up in the near term.

Overall, excluding the WBD deal, I like the company. I like the double-digit growth (both top and bottom line), I like the Street's projections for the next 2 years, and I like the momentum in the ads business. Until I see clarity on the funding of the WBD deal, I believe there is further risk of downside ahead. For now, I rate Netflix as a sell.

A Decent Q3 Quarter

The big headline of Q3 was the one-time $619M Brazilian tax expense, which cut operating margin to 28.2% vs prior guidance of 31.5%. Shares were down double digits the day after earnings. I believe many investors bought the dip. Why?

The growth story remained mostly intact. Despite the Brazilian tax expense, management was able to reaffirm the full-year revenue targets for 2025 (and only reduced 1ppt to the earlier 30% operating margin target). Here is the full guidance from the Q3 shareholder letter.

The momentum in ad sales is stronger than ever. During the last earnings call, co-CEO Greg Peters told investors:

We recorded our best ad sales quarter ever. We are now on track to more than double ad revenue this year.

Unfortunately, the company doesn't disclose advertising revenue in its financial reporting. All we have is a recent disclosure that its ad-supported content now reaches over 190M monthly active viewers across 12 countries, versus roughly 94M ad-tier users in H1 2025. Furthermore, the last 10K shows that ad-supported tier accounts were 55% of new sign-ups in countries where it’s available. I believe that figure is considerably higher now. Why?

According to estimates from JPMorgan, ad revenue is rising from approximately $1.4B in 2024 to $2.9B in 2025 and potentially $4.2B in 2026. If true, the growth rate in this segment this year could be in the triple digits. Not bad, as a growth driver. However, I'm not bullish on Netflix. Let me explain why.

The WBD Deal

The market didn't like the acquisition of WBD’s Studio and Streaming business. Don't take it from me; take it from the price action following news or reports regarding the WBD acquisition.

On November 19, Netflix was down 2.7% after Morgan Stanley’s Benjamin Swinburne questioned the value of the WBD acquisition (mainly due to deal complexity and regulatory hurdles).

On December 3, the stock was down mid-single digits after some reports suggested that the acquisition wouldn't necessarily add many subscribers to Netflix. Here is what Reuters said:

But the sources familiar with the matter said the potential combination of the two services is unlikely to radically expand its market share because the vast majority of HBO Max customers also subscribe to Netflix.

On December 4, sources suggested that Netflix is the top bidder for WBD. The share price was (again) down low single digits.

Finally, on December 5 (while writing this article), the company officially announced the WBD acquisition for $27.75 per share, equivalent to a total enterprise value of approximately $82.7 billion and an equity value of $72.0 billion. The stock is down close to 3% at the time of writing this article.

The earlier reports suggested that Netflix was offering a $5B breakup fee if the deal was turned down by regulators. The expected acquisition price was rumored to be around $28 per share, which means approximately $70B in equity value. The actual results came in quite close to these rumored figures. Furthermore, some sources suggested that the Netflix offer could consist of 85% cash (approximately $60B). Once again, the rumors were close to the final figures.

Under the terms of the agreement, each WBD shareholder will receive $23.25 in cash and $4.501 in shares of Netflix common stock for each share of WBD common stock outstanding at the closing of the transaction. The transaction values Warner Bros. Discovery at $27.75 per share, implying a total equity value of approximately $72.0 billion and an enterprise value of approximately $82.7 billion

In my view, some investors are panicking due to the size of the deal and the limited upside potential. First, the latest 10-Q shows $9.33B in cash + cash equivalents + restricted cash + short-term investments. On top of that, the company has an undrawn $3B revolver and a $3B commercial paper program, with total debt ascending to $14.5B. Assuming the company maxes out its revolver and commercial paper program and completely depletes its liquidity, there is still a gap of $45B ($60B for the cash portion of the WBD acquisition - $15.33B in liquidity, maxing out short-term debt) that needs to be funded with long-term debt.

On top of that, another concern comes from a buyback perspective. The company bought back $7B worth of shares in the first 9 months of 2025, with $10.1B still authorized. The issue that I see here is that the buyback program could be paused (or significantly reduced) after the WBD acquisition.

Finally, the company forecasted $9B in FCF for FY 2025. Again, if the deal goes through, I believe the company will use a good chunk of its FCF to fund the acquisition.

So, do I think Netflix is getting a good deal?

Management certainly believes so. However, the only numerical figure in the section where they discuss the upside of the deal is from a cost savings perspective ($2B - $3B per year).

I want growth. Netflix is a growth stock. I need top-line growth first, then EPS expansion.

In the latest guidance, WBD expected its Streaming business to generate at least $1.3B of Adjusted EBITDA in 2025, and Studios to exceed the prior guidance of at least $2.4B of adjusted EBITDA. From a growth perspective, I am not excited, though. Streaming growth was flat last quarter, and I haven't seen double-digit growth in this segment in the last year.

The Studios segment seems stronger, but it is also very volatile.

In comparison, here is the growth of Netflix's streaming revenue.

Overall, I don't see enough upside in the WBD acquisition. Paying $72B for a slow streaming revenue (where many subscribers are already subscribed to Netflix) and a volatile Studios business is not a good deal, in my view. Now that the acquisition has gone through, interest expenses are likely to increase considerably (considering the size of the deal), likely impacting the bottom line of the company and its share buyback program. I recommend keeping a close eye on the next earnings conference call, as analysts will double down on the company's plans to fund this acquisition.

Valuation

Netflix is trading at 41x next year's earnings. That's about 130% above the median of the communication services. Is this multiple a lot?

Well, I compared Netflix with its peers below.

What I found quite impressive is the fact that WBD is trading at 67x next year's earnings. So, Netflix, which is already expensive, has acquired an even richer company on a forward P/E basis. Overall, I don't think Netflix is an attractive value play.

Conclusion

If it weren't for the potential WBD acquisition, I would rate Netflix as a strong buy. Why?

First, take a look at the chart below.

Shares are testing a multi-year historical trend line. Each time the stock tested this trend, it bounced quite abruptly. Ex-WBD acquisition, I like the fundamentals of the company. Q3 revenue increased by 17.2%, and operating income was up 11.7% YOY. Both the top and bottom lines of the company are expanding by double digits, and the Street expects double-digit growth to persist until the end of 2027.

From a guidance perspective, for Q4 2025, management guided revenue up 16.7% YOY to $11.96B and EPS up 28% YOY to $5.45. They further reiterated FY2025 revenue expectations at $45.1B (near the top of the prior $44.8–45.2B range) and 29% operating margin despite the Q3 hit due to the one-time $619M Brazilian tax expense that cut operating margins in Q3.

Given that we don't know yet how the cash part of the WBD acquisition will be funded ($60B, according to my math), my rating on Netflix is a Sell until this is clear.

data.title: Snowflake Vs. Palantir: Only One Deserves A More Than 200x P/E
data.description:
Snowflake Inc. (SNOW) and Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR) have emerged as software’s strongest AI darlings in the past year. Despite both playing mission critical roles in enabling the ongoing AI transformation, they tackle different functions of the tech stack.

Specifically, Snowflake is currently a leader in the data infrastructure and governance layer. The company’s product pipeline is optimized to help customers centralize and manage their data across clouds, on-prem and hybrid architectures – essentially a “data strategy” that’s emerged as a critical precursor to any AI strategy.

Meanwhile, Palantir operates closer to the application level. Its proprietary ontology-based operating system solutions (e.g., Foundry, Gotham, AI Platform) are built on the underlying data infrastructure that Snowflake's solutions are designed to manage. By consolidating data that’s often fragmented across different systems and functions across an organization, Palantir’s operating systems drive real-time data analytics and insight to empower decision-making across commercial and government end-markets.

In our last coverage on Snowflake in May 2024, we had highlighted how its expanding AI/ML product pipeline at the time – which included the deployment of Cortex, Document AI, and Snowpark – was structurally positioning the company into a more direct participant in emerging data opportunities from enterprise AI adoption. Our expectations for an ensuing positive re-rate at the time has since materialized. This is evident in the stock’s 76% upsurge since our last coverage before it pared some of the gains in the past month.

Meanwhile, our latest coverage on Palantir last month continues to highlight how the stock’s upsurge was supported by more than just AI sentiment, but rather tangible improvements to the unit economics of its recently introduced AIP product. This was evidenced in Palantir’s accelerating government and commercial segment growth in recent quarters, with accompanying margin expansion and earnings accretion underpinned by significant scale achieved through its breakthrough go-to-market strategy.

We’re now revisiting both names in a comparative analysis, as both have re-emerged as two of the most expensive stocks in not just the software cohort, but also the broader market. Snowflake is currently trading at about 221x estimated earnings, while Palantir’s at about 243x despite both stocks’ recent pullbacks. Specifically, Snowflake’s suffered from a post-earnings selloff, despite reporting a strong fiscal Q3 beat and raise earlier this week. Meanwhile, Palantir’s premium has also moderately scaled back in the past month in tandem with broader volatility observed across the broader AI basket.

In the following analysis, we will assess the expectations that market is currently pricing into each of Snowflake and Palantir’s valuation premiums. Then, we will further discuss which of these two enterprise AI software darlings’ valuations offer greater durability at more than 200x estimated P/E today.

Risk of Diverging Valuations Despite Shared AI Tailwinds

Snowflake has reported a strong Q3 FY 2026 beat and raise earlier this week, highlighting continued execution consistency in capturing emerging AI tailwinds. Yet the stock delivered a steep post-earnings selloff. The contradicting market reaction brings to light the growing disconnect between Snowflake’s underlying business fundamentals and investors’ diminishing confidence over the durability of its current valuation premium at about 221x estimated earnings.

Meanwhile, software peer Palantir continues to trade largely in line with its long-term growth trajectory and earnings accretion profile, as discussed in our previous coverage. The stock’s entanglement in the broader AI-driven selloff over the past month has only further de-risked its premium at about 243x estimated earnings, which we believe strengthens its valuation’s durability at current levels.

We believe the core driver of this divergence between Palantir and Snowflake lies in the scalability of both companies’ underlying business models, despite their positioning as key beneficiaries of emerging secular AI tailwinds.

On one hand, Palantir’s effective land-and-expand business model, coupled with robust scalability from its proprietary “AI Platform Bootcamp” (or “AIP Bootcamp”) go-to-market strategy remains a key differentiator. The combination underscores a durable pathway towards sustained operating leverage improvements, monetization efficiency, and earnings accretion for Palantir.

Meanwhile, on the other hand, Snowflake’s latest earnings results and forward earnings guidance continue to reinforce uncertainty over its long-term earnings accretion trajectory – particularly over the extent of its long-term margin expansion prospects, and more importantly, the timing of which the company will achieve GAAP profitability. This remains a key overhang that the Snowflake stock’s currently valuation multiple premium has yet to de-risk for.

Why Snowflake and Palantir Begs Comparison

As mentioned earlier, Snowflake and Palantir have both re-emerged as two of the most expensive names across the software peer group, as well as the broader markets. Their lofty premiums continue to reflect robust market confidence in their respective growth accretive prospects in the AI era. This is corroborated by both businesses’ unique positioning as key beneficiaries of emerging growth opportunities stemming from ongoing enterprise AI adoption.

Specifically, Snowflake plays a mission-critical role in managing the data infrastructure and governance layer – a key precursor to enterprise AI deployments. This is corroborated by CEO Sridhar Ramaswamy’s repeated emphasis that “there is no AI strategy without a data strategy,” highlighting Snowflake’s enabling role in related developments.

Meanwhile, Palantir’s prescient focus in the development of AI-driven data analytic analytics and its proprietary ontology-based enterprise operating systems has also made it a critical component across the AI strategies employed in both the private and public sectors. It solutions have increasingly underpinned mission-critical workflows, particularly in enterprise AI deployments, as well as defense tech stack modernization efforts.

Both companies’ shared fundamental momentum is evidenced from their consistent execution and shared operational resilience. Snowflake’s latest fiscal Q3 beat and raise mirrors similar achievements at Palantir over the same period – both companies have delivered consecutive quarters of earnings outperformance, alongside raised growth outlooks in their respective fiscal year-to-dates.

Resilience in end-market demand is further corroborated by strong enterprise IT spending intentions towards software purchases. Based on industry’s latest CIO survey, 96% of respondents have indicated intentions to increase forward software spending, which represents a significant increase from 71% in 2024. Data functions – such as warehousing, analytics and database management solutions – were prioritized spending areas, highlighting strong secular demand tailwinds for both of Snowflake and Palantir’s current offerings.

The combined demand dynamics, alongside both companies’ consistent delivery of fundamental outperformance, have emerged as key drivers behind their respective stocks’ ballooning valuation premiums this year. Their mission critical role in the ongoing AI transformation, coupled with consistent execution have made them natural comparables as investors evaluate their exposure in high-growth enterprise AI software opportunities.

Snowflake – Understanding Market’s Expectations

Snowflake’s steady recovery this year, as evidenced by the stock’s more than 68% year-to-date gains, has restored its reputation as one of the most expensive at 221x estimated earnings. This compares closely with Palantir’s 243x estimated earnings multiple – the highest within the broader software peer group. Yet Palantir is currently profitable on a GAAP basis, while Snowflake is on track towards widening losses this year. The average consensus estimate also expects Palantir to expand its earnings growth at an average of 51.8% through calendar 2027, which is higher than Snowflake’s 40.6%.

On a proportionate basis, the comparison reflects a hefty premium to Snowflake’s valuation at current levels. Although Palantir isn’t inexpensive either, its current valuation multiple premium is at least supported by tangible scalability. This is substantiated by its expanding margins with ongoing AIP deployment, which is critical for driving sustained cash flow accretion underpinning the stock’s prospects.

To further gauge the durability of Snowflake’s premium valuation at current levels, we’ve performed a reverse discounted cash flow (“DCF”) analysis. This is similar to the exercise we’d performed on Palantir in October to gauge the extent of the long-term growth and earnings expectations that market has currently priced into the stock.

Key valuation assumptions applied to the reverse DCF analysis on Snowflake include a 3.5% perpetual growth rate applied to its projected steady-state cash flows, proxied by FY 2036 EBITDA, to derive the terminal value. The 3.5% assumption is in line with the anticipated long-term economic expansion across Snowflake’s core operating regions, and reflects the sustainable pace at which its steady-state cash flows can grow over the longer-term. The reverse DCF analysis also considers an 8.1% WACC, which aligns with Snowflake’s capital structure and risk profile. The assumption is used to discount the cash flow projections and estimated terminal value over a 10-year forecast period in determining Snowflake’s enterprise value. Snowflake’s net debt is added back to determine the estimated intrinsic value of its cash flow projections.

The underlying cash flow projections used in the reverse DCF analysis for Snowflake assumes quarterly improvements in cost of product revenue towards historical levels, as deployment of its newly introduced AI portfolio scales. Although management guides adjusted operating margin compression in the current quarter, our reverse DCF analysis also estimates gradual operating leverage improvements over the long-term – especially as stock-based compensation (“SBC”) as a percentage of total revenue start to scale. Under these assumptions, the stock’s post-earnings price of about $240 per share (December 4) implies a multi-year revenue and earnings growth profile of at least 25% and 83% (from GAAP net income starting FY 2031), respectively:

*See here for enlarged diagrams throughout: Snowflake_vs._Palantir_-_Supplemental_Graphs.pdf.

Palantir – Understanding Market’s Expectations

We’ve performed a similar reverse DCF analysis for Palantir in the fundamental expectations that currently underpin its market valuation. Specifically, we’ve updated Palantir’s reverse analysis for its actual Q3 earnings results and forward outlook. By maintaining the key valuation assumptions discussed in the previous coverage largely unchanged (i.e., terminal value based on 3.5% perpetual growth rate applied on projected 2035 EBITDA; 9.0% WACC), Palantir’s current price at $176 per share continues to imply a multi-year revenue growth trajectory of at least 34% and earnings accretion trajectory of at least 40%:

Palantir’s latest Q3 earnings results and forward outlook continues to exceed the underlying fundamental assumptions implied from the reverse DCF analysis by wide margins. This accordingly provides ample cushion against inevitable moderation over the longer-term. The implied fundamental trajectory embedded in Palantir’s current valuation premium is further supported by structural AI secular tailwinds, which the company remains a key beneficiary of given its increasingly differentiated scalability advantage. Taken together, we expect durability to Palantir’s market valuation at current levels. There’s also potential for a further uplift, as its impending earnings results are likely to outperform the implied multi-year top- and bottom-line growth expectations of 35% and 41%, respectively.

Snowflake Vs. Palantir – The Divergence Lies In Scalability

What Went Wrong In Snowflake’s Q3 FY 2026 Beat and Raise?

Scalability Uncertainties

Snowflake’s guided FY 2026 growth is largely consistent with the implied growth trajectory from the reverse DCF analysis above. However, the key disappointment comes in management’s guidance for non-GAAP operating margin compression in the current quarter.

Specifically, management guides non-GAAP operating margin of 7% in the current quarter, which represents a four-point compression from 11% during the fiscal third quarter and two-point compression from 9% in the prior year. This represents a significant reversal of the 450-basis point y/y expansion observed in fiscal Q3, driven by ongoing internal efficiencies and better-than-expected growth in recent quarters. Management also provided a vague answer when asked about the underlying drivers of margin compression in the current quarter, furthering concerns over the scalability of Snowflake’s accelerating AI business:

Daniel Knauff - Deutsche Bank AG, Research Division

And then maybe one for Brian, just on operating margins. I think 4Q operating margin was guided maybe a couple of points below where you guided 3Q and maybe a little down from what was implied in the guide last quarter. Anything just to unpack on op margins into Q4 for us to think about as we build our models.

Brian Robins - Chief Financial Officer

Yes. Just real quickly on the 4Q guidance. All I would say is that 4Q is a little tricky in the sense that you're giving the 4Q guidance and the annual guidance at the same time. And so don't read too much into that.

Source: Snowflake F3Q26 Earnings Call Transcript.

The stronger-than-expected non-GAAP operating margin compression for the current quarter also deviates from earlier expectations of F2H26 earnings improvement, given management’s repeated reminder that they’ve intentionally front-loaded Snowflake’s “sales and marketing hiring in the year.” This was further corroborated by its continually elevated sales and marketing spend, which accelerated to 36% of revenue ex-SBC in fiscal Q3 compared to 35% in fiscal Q2. SBC attributable to sales and marketing spend also accelerated to 9.1% of revenue during fiscal Q3, up from 8.8% in fiscal Q2.

The anticipated margin compression also contradicts Snowflake’s accelerating ramp of its AI opportunity capture. Specifically, the company grew its annual recurring revenue run-rate for AI sales of $100 million during the fiscal third quarter. Management’s also disclosed that “Snowflake Intelligence” – Snowflake’s proprietary enterprise AI agent that’s natively integrated in its data cloud platform – has become the fastest-ramping generally available product in the company’s history. Yet impending margin compression suggests potential near-term scale headwinds, raising uncertainties over its AI-driven earnings prospects. The concern is also reflected in Snowflake’s maintained margin targets for FY 2026, despite the $51 million raise in full year product revenue guidance following three consecutive quarters of outperformance.

Taken together, Snowflake’s recent earnings outperformance has done little in improving visibility into the company’s long-term earnings inflection prospects. Uncertainties over its transition to GAAP profitability, which reinforces long-term cash flow accretion and valuation durability, is further compounded by risks of elevated SBC spend. Although Snowflake’s SBC spend as a percentage of revenue (38%) has dropped to its lowest in the past three years during fiscal Q3, it remains significantly elevated on a comparative basis to the software peer average. It also remains much higher than Palantir’s current SBC spend at 16% of its revenue, and exceeds the 21% observed on the eve of Palantir’s GAAP operating and net income inflection in 2023.

The majority of improvements in Snowflake’s current SBC spend came from R&D, general and administrative, and cost of sales functions. Meanwhile, SBC spend attributable to sales and marketing actually increased at a faster pace than revenue growth during the same period.

This contradicts expectations for gradual selling cost improvements, especially as Snowflake expects lower hiring in F2H26 and increases reliance on third-party distribution channels. For instance, Snowflake’s been successful with its integration into AWS Marketplace (AMZN), which gives it access to a massive prospective customer base given the cloud service provider’s leading market share. Specifically, the company disclosed it’s acquired $2 billion in sales through AWS Marketplace within calendar 2025, which doubled from the prior year. Taken together, Snowflake’s cost structure suggests higher sales and customer acquisition spend ahead, as it deepens expansion of its AI-related revenue share gains.

Diminishing Growth Durability

And although Snowflake’s fiscal Q3 growth of 29% and guided FY 2026 growth of 28% remains in line with the long-term CAGR currently priced into the stock, a deeper dive into the composition reveals durability risks that shouldn’t be overlooked.

Specifically, Snowflake’s FY 2026 year-to-date growth continues to decelerate from levels observed in the past two years, despite immediate AI tailwinds. A deeper dive into its RPO dynamics also show ongoing y/y deceleration, despite a strong AI product deployment ramp-up as management’s disclosed.

Specifically, Snowflake’s AI portfolio currently drives half of its new bookings acquired in fiscal Q3. Yet existing customer uptake of Snowflake’s AI use cases are only at 28%, despite four 9-figure expansion deals acquired in the quarter. This suggests Snowflake remains focused on the “landing” phase of its broader land-and-expand business model, which risks limiting near-term expansion efficiency. Not only does this introduce greater uncertainty over Snowflake’s ability in achieving multi-year growth in the 27% range, but it also risks adding pressure to its AI go-to-market strategy, which is already weighing on its profitability.

Why Palantir’s Premium is Comparatively De-Risked and More Durable

Meanwhile, Palantir’s latest earnings outperformance and fundamental outlook reaffirms durability to the multi-year revenue and earnings growth trajectory currently priced into the stock.

Specifically, Palantir delivered revenue growth of 64% y/y during Q3, which implies full year 2025 revenue expansion at 54% y/y. This is accompanied by sustained margin expansion, driven by increased cost of sales efficiency, operating leverage improvements, and ongoing reduction in SBC spend. The combination diverges from deceleration across Snowflake’s forward growth metrics, while also promising sustained margin expansion to support its current P/E multiple premium. With Palantir’s forward outlook exceeding the multi-year expectations currently embedded in the stock’s valuation, we believe its multiple premium remains intact despite recent volatility in market’s AI sentiment.

The sustainability of Palantir’s current fundamental trajectory is primarily reinforced by the scalability of its core AIP sales. Specifically, AIP’s bootcamp-style go-to-market strategy has fundamentally reshaped its unit economics, while accelerating the velocity of new revenue share gains. Specifically, the AIP Bootcamps continue to prove real-time value creation for prospective customers, effectively driving conversion of pilots into long-term contract wins. The AIP Bootcamps’ group-based onboarding also optimizes Palantir’s return on sales spend. This has accordingly unlocked a significant scale advantage for Palantir, despite AIP, Foundry and Gotham being among the most highly customized enterprise AI platforms available today.

Compared to Snowflake, Palantir’s operating model continues to deliver a much more competitive ROI trajectory. Not only is Palantir’s return on AI sales spend much more scalable, as evidenced in sustained improvements on its SBC and sales costs as a percentage of revenue, but Palantir’s also landing new logo wins, while expanding existing customer contracts much more efficiently. This accordingly reinforces tangible visibility into Palantir’s earnings accretion prospects currently embedded in its premium P/E multiple.

Palantir’s growth prospects also continue to outperform the implied trajectory suggested by its current valuation premium. This accordingly keeps its current P/E multiple premium at 243x much more durable than Snowflake’s 221x, as the latter’s outlook remains pressured by signs of extended revenue and backlog deceleration.

Specifically, Palantir’s U.S. Commercial segment continues to be the primary beneficiary of AIP’s scalability, with rapid penetration into opportunities across a wide range of mission-critical verticals spanning industrial, healthcare and financial services. The Government segment also benefits from structural tailwinds, such as increasing prioritization of tech stack modernization initiatives across public sector agencies. This long-term momentum is further reinforced by Palantir’s deepening expansion into allied government opportunities, particularly in Europe, while also increasing the company’s exposure to contracts beyond its core defense and intelligence footholds.

Although the stock has yet to recover from the recent pullback in tandem with broader market volatility observed across AI-focused names, the correction has only further de-risked impending headwinds. They include exposure to rapidly evolving enterprise uptake dynamics due to looming macroeconomic uncertainty, its inherent sensitivity to lumpy government procurement cycles, and risks of competition. It’s also important to note that none of these represent idiosyncratic challenges to Palantir’s current business model and proprietary ontology moat. This dynamic accordingly reinforces Palantir’s ability in achieving the foundational assumptions currently underpinning its valuation premium over the longer-term.

Snowflake – Fundamental and Price Analysis

Adjusting our previous forecast for Snowflake’s latest earnings results and forward outlook, we expect the company to grow FY 2026 revenue at 28% y/y to $4.7 billion. And over the longer-term, we expect the company to grow its revenue at a 21% 10-year CAGR through FY 2036. This will be primarily driven by product revenue growth, with embedded expectations of sustained expansion in the mid- to high-20% range through the end of the decade, as data solution demand driven by industry’s broader AI transition accelerates.

Under the base case assumptions, Snowflake isn’t expected to achieve GAAP profitability until FY 2031. This is consistent with our foregoing analysis on elevated sales and personnel costs as Snowflake prioritizes AI-related revenue share gains, which risks extended its earnings and underlying free cash flow accretion trajectory.

Considering the same key valuation assumptions applied in the reverse DCF analysis, our base case projections yield a price target of $136 per share.

The implied downside of more than 40% from the stock’s current market price of $240 per share (December 4) is consistent with Snowflake’s current P/E premium on a relative basis to its software peers. Snowflake’s estimated forward earnings growth of 43.4% is currently about 1.3x higher than the software peer group average of 19.1%. Applying this proportional outperformance to the peer average P/E of 52.4x yields an implied multiple of 118.7x for Snowflake, which represents close to 50% downside from the stock’s current levels.

Palantir – Fundamental and Price Analysis

Meanwhile, we expect much higher durability to Palantir’s current valuation multiple premium, as its forward growth and earnings accretion trajectory continues to outpace market’s underlying assumptions by wide margins. This is further reinforced by continued execution consistency, as growth acceleration continues to be accompanied by sustained margin expansion, delivering tangible FCF accretion that underpins the stock’s upside potential.

Looking ahead, we expect Palantir to finish the year with 55% y/y revenue growth to $4.5 billion, driven primarily by strong acceleration in commercial segment sales, alongside resilient government AI adoption. And over the longer-term, the company’s AI monetization strategy remains well-positioned in capturing top-line expansion at a five-year CAGR of 44.2% through 2030 and ten-year CAGR of 33.2% through 2035.

This growth trajectory is expected to be accompanied by significant scalability and unlock substantial operating leverage improvements to drive sustained margin expansion. The company is expected to deliver earnings accretion at a ten-year CAGR of at least 39% through 2035, which is in line with current estimates embedded in its valuation premium.

By applying the same key valuation assumptions discussed in the earlier reverse DCF analysis for Palantir (i.e., 3.5% perpetual growth rate applied on projected 2035 EBITDA; 9.0% WACC), the fundamental projections yield a price target of $180 per share. This is largely in line with the stock’s currently traded levels, highlighting resilience to its premium.

As discussed in the earlier section, Palantir’s recent selloff has also de-risked some of the emerging industry headwinds, such as potential competitive headwinds and macro-driven spending contractions. Given this understanding, should Palantir’s upcoming earnings results continue to outperform the multi-year growth projections implied by its current valuation, the stock could be positioned for further upside potential from current levels. And Palantir remains well-positioned to do so, given continued acceleration in its remaining performance obligations (“RPO”), which differs from both revenue and backlog deceleration observed at Snowflake. Specifically, Palantir grew its RPO by 68% y/y to $2.6 billion in Q3, accelerating from 66% y/y in Q2. Growth in total remaining deal value has also remained consistent at more than 90% in recent quarters, totaling $8.6 billion at the end of Q3. Both growth metrics continue to reinforce the long-term earnings accretion trajectory implied by its current valuation by wide margins, which suggests incremental upside potential for Palantir.

Final Thoughts

Taken together, we believe Snowflake’s market valuation following the stock’s year-to-date upsurge has yet to sufficiently de-risk for impending execution challenges – particularly in scaling its AI-driven assumption and translating it into long-term earnings visibility. The uncertainty over its earnings inflection is further compounded by intensifying competition, as evidenced by the increasingly significant and direct overlap between Snowflake and Databricks’ data offerings in recent years. This contrasts from Palantir’s strengthening moat, anchored by its proprietary ontology foundation, and rapid time-to-value and scalability advantages delivered through its innovative bootcamp go-to-market strategy. The divergence also underscores the relative weakness in Snowflake’s 221x P/E premium when compared against Palantir’s 243x.

Admittedly, there are risks to our comparative analysis between Snowflake and Palantir. For instance, the data-first dynamics critical for supporting industry’s AI transformation remain a key growth driver for Snowflake. This is corroborated by its stable net revenue retention of 125%, and a consumption-based revenue model that stands to benefit from increasing AI workloads.

However, we remain incrementally cautious over Snowflake’s costly transition towards its AI product roadmap as a core growth driver. There’s currently limited evidence of scalable AI monetization at Snowflake, especially considering management’s guidance for meaningful margin compression in the current quarter, despite continued product revenue outperformance. This accordingly tempers credibility around its path to GAAP profitability, which represents an overhanging multiple-compression risk.

As a result, we believe Snowflake’s valuation at current levels remains exposed to risks of further recalibration. Meanwhile, Palantir’s durability remains reinforced by continued demonstration of tangible execution consistency in its underlying operations.

data.title: U.S. Total Nonfarm Employment In November 2025
data.description:
According to Revelio Labs' public labor statistics data, seasonally adjusted total nonfarm employment in the U.S. fell in November 2025. The firm's estimate of nonfarm employment is 159,231,610 for the month, down about 9,000 from its revised estimate of 159,240,592 for October 2025. Compared to November 2024, however, the estimate for November 2025 is up nearly 208,000.

Revelio Labs' data indicates total nonfarm employment peaked in April 2025 at 195,280,995 and has largely been slightly lower to flat in the period since. November 2025's estimate is within 0.03% of that peak.

Meanwhile, the BLS has released employment data through September 2025, putting its initial estimate of seasonally adjusted total nonfarm employment at 159,626,000 for the month, which represents a peak in its data series. This value is up 119,000 from the BLS' revised estimate of 159,507,000 for August 2025 and is almost 370,000 higher than Revelio Labs' estimate for September 2025.

Before we go any further, Revelio Labs appears to have executed a major revision of its historical data, with large negative changes to its oldest data that taper off to very small downward changes for the most recent months. The following chart captures both the BLS' and Revelio Labs' latest estimates of seasonally adjusted total nonfarm employment over the period from January 2022 through November 2025 and all Revelio Labs' estimates released in October, November, and December 2025.

Analyst's Notes

At this writing, Revelio Labs has not produced any statement to account for its large downward revisions in its oldest data, which are substantial. In our featured chart, the firm's November 2025 estimate for January 2022 was reduced by over 1.6 million from its October 2025 estimate. Even so, the firm's estimate for January 2022 is over 1.9 million higher than the BLS' estimate for the month.

Without an explanation, we caution against using Revelio Labs' historical data in other analyses. We do observe that Revelio Labs' data does generally match the direction of employment changes in the BLS' historical series over the period covered in our chart, so it's still likely useful for divining that aspect of how the total nonfarm employment level is changing over time.

The Bureau of Labor Statistics indicates it will next release its employment situation data on Tuesday, 16 December 2025, which will include newly released estimates for both October and November 2025.

References

Revelio Labs. Total Nonfarm Employment National. [CSV Data]. 4 December 2025.

U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. Total Nonfarm Employment. Current Employment Statistics - CES. [Online database]. Last Updated 20 November 2025.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The Reasons Why I Find American Electric Power Attractive Right Now (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
American Electric Power (AEP) an electric utility company that generates, transmits, and distributes electricity to customers in the United States.

I started covering the firm back in June 2023 with an initial hold rating, which I kept ever since. In both of the articles I articulated the attractiveness of the business and the strength of its fundamentals, however valuation appeared to be too high in both cases, preventing me to assign a buy rating to the stock at those times.

Year-to-date, however, AEP stock performed exceptionally well. The firm's market cap increased by as much as 28%, outperforming both the S&P500 (SPY) and the utilities sector (XLU) as a whole.

The aim of my article today is to give an updated view on the firm, and highlight the key reasons, why I believe AEP's stock could be an attractive buy in the current market environment. My focus will be on the macroeconomic landscape, on company fundamentals and valuation.

Macro and microeconomic landscape

Consumer

The current economic situation in the United States and globally can be described as uncertain, unpredictable and volatile. This is clearly visible from a set of economic indicators that we like to use to gauge the health of the economy. One of these indicators is consumer confidence, which is often used as a leading economic indicator to gauge how the spending behaviour of the consumer is likely to change in the near term. Consumer confidence in the U.S. is hovering around its historic lows, meaning that people have significant concerns about their financial health and outlook. This could lead to less and less spending on durable, non-essential, discretionary goods.

AEP's advantage in this landscape, however, is that its not directly impacted by the sentiment of the consumer. Regardless of the people's financial outlooks, the demand for energy and electricity is not likely to substantially fall. This is one of the primary reasons, I believe AEP could be an attractive investment right now.

Interest rates

AEP's business is very capital intensive, like most utility businesses. And a significant portion of their operation is financed by debt.

When a firm is so debt dependent, naturally their financial results are also very much dependent on the extent of interest expenses, which is driven by the level of the interest rates set by the Fed. The Fed is going to be meeting on the 10th of December to discuss whether interest rates will be lowered once again this year or not. The outcome of this meeting can lead to significant volatility not just in AEP's stock, but also in the whole market. If interest rates were to remain high, many firms that are financing their AI build outs by debt could faces difficulties. Further, cost savings and further job cuts could lead to further increasing unemployment. I see this as the number one near term risk that we need to keep an eye on.

If we look a bit further, AEP also shared its financing plan till 2030, which is heavily reliant on debt. Therefore, the level of interest rates will largely impact their cost of capital and profitablity.

Data centers
