As more and more companies are participating in the AI race, the demand for data centers keeps growing, and therefore the need for power is also higher than ever. This creates significant growth opportunity for AEP in the mid- to long term.

AEP anticipates operating earnings growth rate at 9% through 2030, with growth starting at a slower rate in the next two years, but increasing in 2028, 2029 and 2030.

I believe that this expectation is realistic and achievable and is in line with the overall growth of the demand for electricity.

Dividends

AEP remains a reliable dividend payer and they put special emphasis on keep returning value to the shareholders. The firm currently pays a quarterly dividend of $0.95 per share, which is roughly equivalent to an annual dividend yield of 3.2%. Looking to the next 5 years, AEP aims to keep the dividend yield around 3%, meaning that if the business grows at a rate of 7% to 9%, we can expect the dividends to grow at the same rate, while keeping the payout ratio at a sustainable level of 50% to 60%.

This could be particularly appealing for dividend- and dividend growth investors.

AEP also has a long history of 36 years of continuous dividend payments, and also they also managed to increase these in each of the last 16 years.

All in all, I believe that the secular tailwinds from the AI infrastructure build out, as well as the independence from the consumer sentiment make AEP an attractive investment opportunity in the current market.

Valuation

No analysis is complete without a valuation section. Today, I will be using a dividend discount model to value AEP's stock. My main input parameters for this evaluation are the:

1.) Dividend growth rate in the short term (until 2030), which I assume to be 7%, based on the firm's expectations.

2.) Perpetual dividend growth rate of 2.5%, equivalent to the overall growth of the U.S. economy, historically.

3.) Required rate of return, which I assume to be equivalent to the firm's cost of equity, 7.1%.

These inputs result in a fair value of $111 per share, which is roughly in line with the current share price of $118.

I believe that at the current price, AEP's stock is fairly valued, based on its dividends.

Conclusions

AEP's business is relatively independent from the consumer sentiment, which makes it an attractive investment option in the current market. Further, the demand for power is forecasted to increase significantly, with the gradual build out of AI infrastructures, including data centers, providing substantial growth opportunity for AEP.

The firm's capital allocation, especially its commitment to dividend payments and dividend growth could make AEP attractive for dividend- and dividend growth investors.

On the other hand, the firm's high reliance on debt, and therefore on the level of interest rates in the coming years, pose substantial risks that investors must keep in mind.

Based on a dividend discount model, AEP stock appears to be fairly valued.

For these reasons, I assign a buy rating to AEP's stock.

data.title: S&P 500 2026 Outlook: Market Multiple Holds As AI Remains The Growth Driver
data.description:
Wins and losses have now become tactile and psychosomatic

Large institutions continue to ratchet up their 2026 estimates for the S&P 500 (NASDAQ: SPY). Going into 2026, we can find a wide compilation of reasons as to why the market 'should' go lower. Oddly, there's no shortage of experts, often from those same institutions, predicting positive returns, hedging their bets by saying lower returns to negative in the years to come, still betting on index increases next year for the S&P 500 (SP500), Nasdaq (QQQ), and Dow (DIA).

Amongst the reasons higher multiples seem to be here to stay:

  • Higher quality companies with bigger margins.
  • Technology making it easier to change your mind.

In the era before computers, you called your advisor to make a trade and found out your results on a quarterly basis sent in the mail. Now the market is becoming ingrained in all of us, and the siren call to buy or sell is at our fingertips daily. You can't escape it.

This is why I say Steve Jobs, creator of the smartphone, may have ruined prolonged bear markets forever. Sure, we'll get them for a year or so, like the pullback in 2022, but if you're betting on a -20% drop sustaining for longer than 18 months before a return back to an all-time high, will you be able to resist and not capitulate with each ding of a buy signal hitting your pocket at the gym as you rack your weights?

All this might not have to do with some sort of deterioration in our logic or lowering of standards. We now function in a world where gains and losses are very tactile. You touch your screen constantly, scrolling charts and placing trades; the feedback makes you feel almost as if you are in control. Scientists have studied this phenomenon, and I believe we'd be foolish to dismiss it.

Smart phone ubiquity

One of the first studies worth reading comes from Beijing University, studying smartphone influences for US and Chinese college students when using a smart phone to trade versus a laptop.

Smartphones have several unique attributes that have the potential to significantly shape users’ behaviors. For example, users are more likely to self-disclose on smartphones than on laptops because smartphone users experience greater psychological comfort and concentrate more on their communication tasks (Melumad & Meyer, 2020). The touchscreens on smartphones have also been argued to induce affect-laden choices such as chocolate cakes (vs. fruit salad) because the sensation of touch mentally simulates interactions with products (Shen et al., 2016).

The current research focuses on another important attribute of smartphones—ubiquity (Okazaki & Mendez, 2013), which refers to how a device can be used almost anytime and anywhere to address various needs.

heavier users of mobile devices show weaker tendencies to delay gratification and greater inclinations toward impulsive behavior (Wilmer & Chein, 2016).

Firstly, the researchers found that smartphones as a medium hurt an individual's ability to delay gratification. This could mean it leads to individuals selling too often [increasing volatility and market standard deviation], but the FOMO factor also enters the picture when sidelined sellers notice a bounce. To stay away from buying or selling is becoming increasingly difficult.

Trading on a phone increases risk-taking

Next we have a study from four academics, two in Germany and two at Indiana University:

Using transaction-level data from two German banks, they studied the effects of smartphones on trading behavior. Comparing trades placed by the same trader in the same month across different platforms, they showed how traders buy riskier assets when they’re trading on a smartphone, particularly so-called lottery stocks. In other words, trading on a phone seems to have a disinhibiting effect.

Smartphone traders, the researchers found, are also more likely to chase performance, buying stocks that have performed well in the recent past.

Furthermore, the research shows, the effect of smartphones does not appear to be short-lived and transitory. Traders who place riskier trades on their phones are more likely to repeat that behavior in the future, regardless of whether they use a phone to trade on or not.

The authors of the paper conclude: “Collectively, our evidence suggests that investors make more intuitive (system 1-type) decisions while using smartphones. This tendency leads to increased risk-taking, gambling-like activity, and more trend chasing.

Similar to the Beijing study that found more hedonic impulses on a phone versus a laptop, these researchers discovered increased levels of risk-taking on a phone versus other mediums. If you throw in new popular platforms like Robinhood (HOOD) that might have haptic feedback, this becomes a psychosomatic touch-to-brain feedback loop that internalizes these habits much more deeply than making decisions at a keyboard attached to a PC that needs to boot up and where you need to log on to your account. Instant power, ubiquity, and always logged on, ready for a trade or to feed you the next buy/sell article or YouTube video of the day. The smartphone is unique.

Touch is the key

This touch is demonstrated even better when it comes to e-commerce, as can be seen in this study:

This work presents initial explorations into the effects of varying touch-based interfaces on consumers, and argues that research into the interfaces used to access content can be as important as research into the content itself. Two laboratory studies using a variety of touch technologies explore how touchscreen interfaces can increase perceived psychological ownership, and this in turn magnifies the endowment effect.

Results highlight that perceptions of online products and marketing activities are filtered through the lens of the interfaces used to explore them, and touch-based devices like tablets can lead to higher product valuations when compared to traditional computers.

While this was focused on e-commerce, it is fascinating that haptic feedback increased mental product valuations. This should apply to the stock market as well. The endowment effect is a problem in financial planning, as investors become attached to an investment due to an inheritance or some other attachment. We can imagine that tactile feedback for some increases risk-taking and for others enhances mental ownership or 'endowment' of that investment. This creates two extremes, extreme risk takers and dogmatic buy-and-holders.

Now let's transition to the fundamental reasons

Smartphones and AI are a big part of the story, in my opinion, influencing retail and, I'm sure, greatly influencing institutional hedonistic tendencies as fund managers become younger and younger. Big money and small money traders are exposed to the same noise. The fundamental reasons the market still has 2-3 years left in this bull run are numerous.

Where are we at in a historical context for the S&P 500?

First, on reasons why the market multiple will hold, let's first see where we sit on the forward P/E versus history:

At around 23X forward, this is close to the 2021 peak. Even though approaching all-time highs in the market once again in this first week of December, multiples have contracted a bit with forward earnings being revised higher.

Once the multiple is set, growth determines market outcomes

If the market has now re-rated what it's willing to pay for forward earnings, then the market should follow the growth rate in those earnings. Alternatives are not great. The 10-year yielding 4% or lower in years to come is equivalent to a P/E ratio of 25 or worse if the yield continues to sink.

Real estate

Another competitive asset, commercial real estate, has seen cap rates increase to the high 5% rate. A 5.8% cap rate would be the equivalent of a 17.24 X P/E. Good, but don't forget leverage is often needed here, which is still too expensive. This becomes competitive to stocks possibly as interest rates fall. Stocks are still the least amount of headache for the current yield they offer.

Percentage of earnings beats in 2025

From FactSet:

Overall, 95% of the companies in the S&P 500 have reported actual results for Q3 2025 to date. Of these companies, 83% have reported actual EPS above estimates, which is above the 5-year average of 78% and above the 10-year average of 75%. If 83% is the final number for the quarter, it will mark the largest percentage of S&P 500 companies reporting a positive EPS surprise for a quarter since Q2 2021 (87%).

With the amount of earnings beats at hand, the forward earnings estimates might be low in reality.

The market is already looking to 2027

From economicgreenfield.com:

Data via Refinitiv:

Year EPS ($/share) YoY Growth
2014 118.78 —
2015 117.46 –1.11%
2016 118.10 +0.54%
2017 132.00 +11.77%
2018 161.93 +22.67%
2019 162.93 +0.62%
2020 139.72 –14.25%
2021 208.12 +48.96%
2022 218.09 +4.79%
2023 221.36 +1.50%
2024 242.73 +9.65%
2025 (est.) 270.53 +11.45%
2026 (est.) 307.86 +13.80%
2027 (est.) 350.98 +14.01%

If we're incorporating the 2027 estimate and the current forward multiple of 23X holds, with few competitive asset classes from an ROI perspective, the market should follow that 2027 14% track plus or minus a percent.

AI is not the internet, it's an arms race

Much of this hinges on AI spending and making dollars circulate in the economy via this AI CAPEX spending. AI is often compared to the internet bubble of 1999. The question is, who was the US competing with at that point? No one. AI is an arms race against China, with comparisons to the Manhattan Project or the space race versus Russia.

Money will be spent and subsidized in my opinion no matter what the ROI. The US wants to be the first to achieve AGI and super intelligence thereafter. If you're a journeyman tech CEO, this is the holy grail of achievements that can not be topped in their imaginations.

We are in the debt phase; next comes subsidy

The Mag 7 are very debt-light. Even though debt spending is getting a lot of attention, they are just now beginning to tap their balance sheets. The credit card still has 95% left until it hits the limit. This is the 2026 spending story.

Then in 2027, I believe the subsidy phase will kick in. Debt and free cash flow funded CAEPX will subside and the government will begin to subsidize data centers, chips, energy and infrastructure via tax credits and possibly preferential financing. This gives the spending legs until 2028.

2026 call?

With the S&P 500 at 6,838 as I write this, I believe we end next year near 7,795, 14% to the upside incorporating 2027 numbers. If and when a bear kicks in, [ my bet would be 2028], I still believe the endowment effect of smart devices via tactile and haptic feedback continues to shorten the duration of bear markets. A dovish FED is also thrown into the mix here come 2026 that can both lower short rates and start up QE to really kick everything into overdrive.

data.title: Par Pacific Holdings: A Company With Strong Growth But Already Priced By The Market
data.description:
Par Pacific Holdings (PARR) is a company that refines fossil fuels and operates gas stations in some of the territories where it operates. PARR has operations in Hawaii and along the Pacific Northwest coast of the United States. In addition to Hawaii, the company operates in Washington, Wyoming, and Montana. In Hawaii, the company owns the "Hela" chain of gas stations, and along the Pacific coast, it owns the "Nom Nom" chain of gas stations.

PARR has shown improved results, as we will see in the following segments, which justified part of the company's bullish rally over the past year. However, as I will show in this report, another part of the increase is based on strong future growth for the company.

Thesis

As I mentioned in the introduction, Par Pacific is a company that refines fossil fuels and sells them to the end consumer. The company primarily operates in the northwestern United States, where, in addition to its refineries, it owns the "Nom Nom" chain of gas stations, and in Hawaii, where it owns both the refinery and the "Hele" brand of gas stations.

The company constructs an index for the refineries in each of the states where it operates. This index is based on the highest-yielding combination of each refinery product from a barrel of crude oil, minus the operating costs of producing that distillate with the composition detailed in the index. For more information on how each index is calculated, please refer to the 2025 investor presentation. However, for the purposes of this analysis, it suffices to note that the higher the index price in each state, the better the profitability the company obtains for each barrel of WTI it processes at its various refineries.

What can be observed is that in all distilleries, the values ​​for each index are around the average value of the last 5 years, with the sole exception of Washington, which exceeds it. At the same time, a recovery can be seen in each case compared to the last quarters of the year, indicating a recovery in the company's profitability.

Turning to capacity, the Hawaii refinery is the largest producer, generating the equivalent of 94 MBOE/d, while the Wyoming refinery is the smallest, producing around 20 MBOE/d. ​​The company primarily focuses on the production of gasoline and distillates, which together account for over 73% of total production. The remaining 27% is distributed among LSFO, asphalt, and other products. Therefore, the strategy of operating gas stations in both Hawaii and along the Pacific North Coast makes sense within the company's vertical integration plan.

Another point I'd like to mention is that Par Pacific maintains the best distillate yield compared to other companies that are geographically similar and in terms of production size. While the three comparable companies have a yield of around 40%, Par Pacific has a yield of 52%, which is significantly higher than its competitors. This again demonstrates a better market position, which justifies the company's upward trend over the past year.

Analyzing EBITDA growth, we can see a CAGR increase of approximately 16% from 2021 to the first nine months of this year. Since 2021, the company has grown from $121 million to $216 million this year.

Finally, regarding the company's CAPEX, a significant increase can be observed in 2024 compared to the projected 2025 figure. However, in the future, the value of these capital investments is expected to decrease again, concentrating solely on maintenance and turnaround CAPEX, returning to levels slightly higher than those of 2022 and 2023. Based on the company's forecast, growth CAPEX is not expected to continue; therefore, FCF generation for 2026 could be expected to increase compared to the values ​​of 2024 and 2025.

However, as can be seen below, the company has experienced an upward trend over the past year. This strong increase is primarily driven by the sustained growth in earnings that the company has demonstrated recently. Therefore, from my perspective, as mentioned, the production indices for each state are hovering around the average of the last five years. Furthermore, it is likely that future earnings growth is priced into the company's current upward trend.

3Q2025 Results

In this section, I would like to focus on the results presented by the company in 3Q2025 and compare them with previous quarters. This will allow me to broaden the description of the company's current situation and thus develop a more comprehensive analysis of the previous section.

I'd like to begin by describing the company's revenue performance in this last quarter and in relation to the total accumulated revenue for the first nine months of the year. According to PARR, revenue for the quarter reached $2.01 billion, and the total for the first nine months of the year was $5.65 billion. In both cases, a decrease can be observed compared to the 2024 figures, where 3Q2024 showed a total of $2.14 billion, while the accumulated total was $6.14 billion. Despite these decreases of 6.1% and 8%, respectively, the company showed a decrease in production costs that led to an increase in both EBITDA and Net Income in the end.

If we now turn to analyzing the evolution of EBITDA, we see that in Q3 2025, the value reported by PARR reached $372.5 million, representing a 623% increase compared to the $51.5 million of the previous year. Regarding the first nine months of the year, the EBITDA reported by the company reached $520.5 million, compared to $227.7 million of the previous year.

In this sense, we can see that the third quarter of the year represented a substantial increase compared to the values ​​the company had been reporting. Given this spectacular growth, I don't believe it's appropriate to annualize this latter value; instead, it makes sense, from my point of view, to annualize the nine-month total. In this case, if we take that annualized value and compare it with the company's current EV, we see that the EV/EBITDA ratio is around 5.2x. Regarding this value, while I believe it is attractive, I think it is best to wait and see how the company's results develop to determine if it can maintain these values ​​in the following quarters.

I want to move on to analyzing the reported Cash From Operating Activities. In the last quarter, PARR reported $219.4 million, representing an increase of approximately 180% compared to the $78.5 million reported in the third quarter of 2024. Regarding the year-to-date figures, we see a similar trend. In the first nine months of this year, the value reached $351.6 million, while in the same period last year, the total was $99.2 million. It's worth mentioning that we can again see a significant increase in the last quarter compared to previous periods. If we annualize these amounts and compare them to the company's current market capitalization, we see that the P/CF ratio is around 7.7x.

Finally, to close this section, I would like to mention the deleveraging process the company has successfully implemented. As you can see below, the company has accumulated liquidity while simultaneously reducing its debt-to-EBITDA ratio to the levels management set as its target.

Valuation

In this section, I would like to proceed with a valuation of the company to assess its upside potential compared to the scenario I outlined in the previous sections. To this end, I constructed a DCF model and applied a series of assumptions, which I would like to detail before presenting the results.

Before detailing the assumptions I used to construct the DCF model presented in this section, I want to clarify that I sought to build a margin of safety to provide some confidence in my decision-making based on the results obtained. Therefore, it is possible that some rates may be higher than average and some financial amounts may grow at a slower rate than might be expected.

That said, to begin with the assumptions, I want to mention the 25% tax rate I used for the calculations. This value is slightly above the average for recent years, as seen in the Financials segment of Seeking Alpha, and was calculated this way to maintain the aforementioned safety margin. On the other hand, the perpetual growth rate I used was 2%, which is below the average global economic growth rate of recent decades. This is because the company is involved in oil refining, which is a non-renewable energy source that will eventually run out.

Regarding the WACC, I used a value of 11%, which again falls below the values ​​reported by specialized websites, which are closer to 10%. I believe this is the point with the greatest impact when constructing the safety margin, due to the very nature of the DCF model. For this reason, I decided to use a slightly higher value.

To conclude this section on assumptions, it's worth mentioning the growth rates for EBITDA, depreciation and amortization (D&A), and CAPEX. Given the context described in the previous sections, I decided to assume EBITDA growth of 10% for the first year, 6% for the second, 5% for the third, 3% for the fourth, and 2.5% for the fifth. This then converges to the perpetual growth rate. To simplify the model, I estimated D&A growth of the same magnitude as for EBITDA. Finally, for CAPEX, and also for the sake of simplification, I assumed a constant increase of 1.5% over the five years of the period.

Based on these assumptions, it can be seen that the company is practically trading at fair value, with a potential upside of only 3%. Therefore, the conjecture made in the thesis section regarding the market having already priced in some of the company's projected growth appears to be confirmed. The recent upward rally has already factored in some of this growth potential, leading me to consider the company a HOLD at this time.

Risks

Finally, I'd like to mention the risks associated with this investment. Undoubtedly, as discussed in the valuation and thesis sections, part of the current price and the recent upward trend are based on the company maintaining its performance and continuing to grow at the rates it has shown recently. Therefore, there is a significant risk that if this growth is not as substantial as expected, the company could reverse its recent upward trend.

data.title: American Bitcoin: A High-Flying Mining Story With A Valuation That's Running Out Of Road
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I assign American Bitcoin Corp. (NASDAQ:ABTC) a hold rating because, although the company has strengthening operational momentum, the stock price is already way ahead of its current net asset value. The fleet upgrade that has pushed the company's hashrate to 25 EH/s, along with its rising Bitcoin output and positive EBITDA, shows a company that is moving in the right direction. Nevertheless, the balance sheet remains burdened by miner purchases and lease obligations and, thus, the company's share price reflects the expectations of future expansion rather than the value of the Bitcoin the company currently holds. So, until the company can prove that it can scale toward its current 50 EH/s target while, at the same time, managing its liabilities, its risk-reward profile remains balanced. Therefore, I recommend for investors to wait for a better entry point or for further execution before taking a stronger stance toward the company.

Background & Latest News

American Bitcoin came to market on September 3, 2025 through a merger with Gryphon Digital Mining, and the pitch was a simple one: build one of the most efficient mining fleets in North America, and use those fleets to mine and accumulate a large and long-term Bitcoin (BTC-USD) reserve—a plan that the company has quickly jumped on.

Per the company's latest 10-Q, and as of November 13, 2025, American Bitcoin has accumulated approximately 4,090 Bitcoin in reserve and has the mining power of a cumulative hashrate of 25.0 EH/s, up from its previous 10.0 EH/s, across four sites—all this while upgrading their fleet which resulted in higher efficiency Antminer S21+ miners that, ultimately, improved their Bitcoin mining operations.

Despite these technological improvements, the company's stock has had other plans. While company stock opened trading above $8 back in September, it has since fallen to the $2 range that included a 37% drop in a single day. But this decline wasn't due to any operational issues; rather, the decline was due to selling pressure that was caused by early investors/shareholders selling as their lock-up period expired which, essentially, placed a heavy supply of shares into a thin float and overwhelmed demand. Bitcoin's volatility obviously added to the anxiety, but, in sum, the selling was due to too much supply hitting too little demand as opposed to anything that has to do with the company's fundamentals.

The result of all this selling pressure is a widening gap between the company's growing mining capacity and Bitcoin stack and the market's decreasing view of the company's equity.

Latest Quarterly Results & Critical Financials

American Bitcoin's latest quarter shows a company that is going in one direction: up—even though the stock has been on its way down. Per the latest 10-Q, revenue came in at $64.2 million, up dramatically from last year's $11.6 million. The drivers for this revenue growth was from the previously mentioned fleet upgrade to S21+ miners, which added a cumulative 15 EH/s, and a higher uptime across all sites. And, in total, the company mined 563 Bitcoin in Q3 versus last year's Q3 total of 190 and earned far more per coin due to a more expensive price per Bitcoin.

Cost of revenue also rose to $28.3 million due to a higher energy use and a rise in the price of power. And depreciation more than tripled due to the company buying its larger, more advanced fleet. General and administrative expenses also increased because of the company's one-time merger costs as opposed to something such as a bloated overhead. So, although operating expenses are now elevated, they are not expected to repeat.

I do want to add something that I believe is important: One area of the 10-Q that may have distorted the company's results, and maybe even confused some investors, was the company's loss on digital assets. However, this was more so an accounting issue and not a business issue. What I mean is this: When American Bitcoin became a standalone company, they had to start their Bitcoin reserve from scratch—this coming directly from the company's MD&A that I'll quote below:

On March 31, 2025, in connection with the Transactions, Parent’s strategic Bitcoin reserve remained with Parent. Therefore, immediately following the effectuation of the Transactions on March 31, 2025, the Company had no Bitcoin in reserve. Starting April 1, 2025, the Company began to build its own strategic Bitcoin reserve.

Thus, since the value of these coins are marked to market each quarter, if Bitcoin's price ends the quarter below the level where those coins were added, then the company must record a loss on paper even though the company sold no coins and no cash actually left the business. Thus, in sum, none of this affects the company's cash flow from mining.

On a headline basis, the company reported $3.5 million in net income for the quarter versus last year's Q3 loss, and, to add to that, adjusted EBITDA reached $27.7 million, that essentially flipped the company from its negative territory in 2024 and highlights the operating leverage of the company's upgraded fleet.

In sum, I believe the quarter showed four important qualities every investor should look for in these types of companies: a stronger mining output, a rise in efficiency, clearer margins, and an improved earnings power. Thus, we have a company whose financials are strengthening even though the stock price is falling, thereby widening the gap between fundamental performance and investor perception.

Growth Drivers and Catalysts

The company's path forward is shaped by two primary forces: a mining fleet that is currently scaling rapidly and a Bitcoin market that continues to tighten. Let's start with the mining fleet.

American Bitcoin's Mining Fleet

American Bitcoin now controls not only one of the largest—but also one of the most efficient—mining fleets in North America, one that I have already mentioned has jumped up from its previous 10.0 EH/s to its current cumulative hashrate of 25.0 EH/s after deploying 14.86 EH/s of new Bitmain Mining S21+ units. What these machines allow the company to do is to mine more Bitcoin per watt, thereby giving the company a cost edge while allowing them to accumulate coins in years where network difficulty may arise or the price of Bitcoin wobbles.

Thus, the company's three-fold strategy of improving mining efficiency, avoiding selling, and compounding their reserve has pushed American Bitcoin holdings above 4,000 Bitcoin. On top of that, the company also has the ability to raise cash gradually though its at-the-market [ATM] program—launched in Q3 and noted as one of the company's Q3 2025 highlights in their 10-Q—and, therefore, has the flexibility to keep expanding its fleet without taking on any expensive debt or, especially, selling off its Bitcoin reserves. And management's path to 50 EH/s, if reached, would place the company among the largest publicly listed miners.

A Tightening Bitcoin Market

Meanwhile, the other half of the growth driver story for the company is in the asset American Bitcoin produces. I wrote an article on Bitcoin last month where I mentioned the three major drivers, or forces, that are defining Bitcoin's value going into the future. Thus, I will also relay them here given that these drivers are also important for the company since Bitcoin is their primary asset.

Bitcoin's available supply continues to shrink as long-term holders, ETFs, corporates, and lending markets continue to pull coins out of circulation. More than 73% of Bitcoin is being held long-term, and in-kind redemptions are routing coins into ETF vaults as opposed to exchanges. There are also upcoming spot ETF approvals in South Korea and Japan that would remove even more supply from the market. And, for miners, these structural shifts matter because with fewer coins chasing hands and more coins held in custody, the long-term value of every Bitcoin produced continues to rise.

In Sum

Taken together, American Bitcoin benefits from these two primary forces: its own operational momentum and the tightening of Bitcoin's float. Should the company continue to grow its fleet and reserve at its current pace, then it should stand to capture more upside from Bitcoin's supply-driven cycle than its falling share price is suggesting.

Valuation

Since American Bitcoin is built around accumulating Bitcoin, the easiest way to value the company is by taking a look at where the digital asset stands today. As previously stated, per the latest 10-Q, American Bitcoin has accumulated approximately 4,090 Bitcoin in reserve that is worth around $376.8 million valued at the current Bitcoin price of $92,140. When we add back the company's cash of around $7.98 million, we get a total liquid asset base of $384.82 million; however, we still need to look at the company's obligations.

The latest Q3 balance sheet shows a company with $587.37 million in total liabilities that is driven primarily by miner-purchase obligations and lease liabilities that are tied directly to the company's fleet expansion. Thus, below is a table of the math:

Therefore, on a strict net asset basis, American Bitcoin is deeply overvalued simply because its current liabilities, at least for now, exceed the value of its Bitcoin and cash, thereby proving mathematically that the stock is not trading at its present net worth; rather, the company is trading on the expectations of future mining growth, reserve compounding, and a strong Bitcoin cycle.

Thesis Risk

The biggest risk to American Bitcoin, so far that I can see, is its balance sheet. The company's miner-purchase obligations and lease liabilities loom large relative to the value of the company's Bitcoin and, should Bitcoin's price tumble or even remain flat for a few quarters, the company could inevitably face a tighter cash position while still needing to fund further fleet expansion. And, should there be any delay in reaching its 50 EH/s target, then that could weaken the core argument that the company's future production currently justifies today's equity premium.

A second risk for the company lies in network difficulty. Bitcoin mining difficulty is continuing to rise as there is more capacity coming online globally. If the company's efficiency gains fail to keep pace with these increases in difficulty, then the number of Bitcoins mined per EH/s will fall, thereby pressuring margins and slowing down the company's reserve growth. Even small changes in difficulty can meaningfully alter the economics for any miner operating with large capital commitments.

A third risk for the company lies in stock dilution. The company's at-the-market program gives the company flexibility, but it also means that future expansion could come at the cost of issuing new shares, which would thereby reduce per-share value unless the company's mining output grows faster than its dilution.

And, finally, there are always risks that come from macro conditions. For example, a sharp drop in Bitcoin, a freeze in credit markets, or stress in crypto-linked financial products could, no doubt, reduce liquidity and push investors out of high-beta mining stocks.

Conclusion & Investor Takeaway

To summarize, American Bitcoin is executing well operationally, and it has a larger, more efficient fleet to go along with its rising Bitcoin production. But the stock's sharp disconnect from its net asset value means that investors are, as of right now, paying for growth that still has yet to be proven. The company must scale toward its 50 EH/s target, manage its liabilities, and grow its Bitcoin reserve without leaning heavily on its dilution or a perfect Bitcoin cycle.

As of now, the setup is balanced: There is strong momentum on the operations side, but we also have a valuation that leaves very little margin for error. Thus, until the company shows clearer progression on expansion and balance-sheet strength, American Bitcoin remains a hold. A better entry-point, or even stronger evidence that future mining output will justify the company's current premium, would shift that view.

data.title: Trinity Capital: Despite Risks, 14% Yield Worth Considering
data.description:
There are lots of ways to earn big yield in this market, and the BDC space (business development companies) is ripe with opportunities. And while BDCs come in a wide variety of shapes and sizes (data is shared in this report), one that stands out is Trinity Capital (TRIN) because of its growth-sector niche (arguably better aligned with the “new” US economy), internal management team (less conflicts of interest) and outstanding 14% dividend yield (covered by net investment income and backed by growing book value). This report reviews all of that, plus the big risks Trinity currently faces, and then concludes with my strong opinion on investing.

Overview:

Trinity Capital provides diversified financial solutions to growth-stage companies (i.e. mainly loans, equipment financing and “equity & warrants”), and it operates as an internally managed BDC.

For some background, BDCs were formed by an act of Congress in 1980 to help small (middle market) businesses access capital. As such, BDCs are not taxed at the corporate level as long as they pay out at least 90% of their earnings as dividends. And this is basically the reason so many income-focused investors love BDCs (i.e. the big dividend yields, as you can see in the following table).

And what makes Trinity unique in the BDC space is its focus on innovative technology (such as AI, software and space technologies—as you can see in the earlier graphic) as well as disruptive healthcare sectors of the economy. This separates Trinity from other BDCs (who are often more focused on older economic sectors) and it has also allowed Trinity to post some relatively impressive total returns (dividends—as if they were reinvested—plus price gains) in recent years as compared to other BDCs. You can see a sample of Trinity investment examples in the graphic below.

Also worth mentioning, Trinity is often granted warrants (the right to buy equity at attractive prices in the future) as part of the financing arrangements they provide to these companies; this can serve both parties well (i.e. the company, because they don’t have to immediately give away a dilutive ownership stake in their business, and Trinity because they get some attractive equity upside too—in addition to the attractive rates they are earnings on the loans they provide—as you can see in the graphic below—this is a big part of how they are able to pay investors such a big quarterly dividend yield).

Strong Financial in a Challenging Market

Despite headwinds for BDCs in general (such as economic slowdowns and easing rates—more on this in the risks section of this report), the current market environment has been strong for Trinity. For example, Trinity originated a record $773 million in Q3 commitments, thereby pushing their year-to-date total over $1.3 billion.

Further, total investment income climbed 22.3% year-over-year to $75.6 million, with NII up 29% to $37 million ($0.52/share—which exceeds the $0.51 cent quarterly dividend). Additionally, Trinity is demonstrating prudent credit quality (the BDC maintains an investment grade credit rating) in a risky landscape. For example, Trinity's weighted-average risk rating (its own internal metric) held steady at 2.9 (on a 1-5 scale) through Q3 (reflecting continued discipline in underwriting standards—amid modest sector-wide non-accrual increases). This resilience counters 2025's credit crunch, where other leveraged borrowers have been strained under persistent high rates).

And for a little more perspective, you can see the non-accrual percentages (basically the percent of loans not paying as per their loan terms—i.e. technical default) along with various other important BDCs metrics across the industry (i.e. peers and competitors of Trinity) in the following table (lots of good data here).

Dividend Safety

As mentioned, Trinity’s net investment income continues to cover its quarterly dividend payments to shareholders (a good thing), but it also has a growing history of steady dividend increases (as you can see in the chart below).

Just because a company is increasing its dividend, that is not necessarily a good thing. However, Trinity has been increasing its net asset value (see chart below) and its net investment income too, which does lend support to the attractiveness of this big-dividend BDC.

Valuation:

Currently trading at approximately 1.1x book value, Trinity is fairly reasonably priced compared to its own history (see earlier BDC table for range) and peers, especially considering the aggregate BDC book value keeps growing (while book value per share is also trending slightly higher).

And the eight Wall Street analysts covering Trinity rate it a “Buy” (with a 1.89 aggregate rating, and 10.4% share priced upside) as you can see in our earlier BDC comparison table.

Trinity Risks:

However, Trinity also faces risks investors should consider. For example, primary concerns include:

Interest Rates are a risk factor investors need to consider. In particular, Trinity has an 82.4% floating rate debt investment portfolio and a 40.6% floating rate borrowing portfolio (as of September 30, 2025). And with uncertainty surrounding the rate (and even direction) of upcoming Fed interest rate moves, Trinity is at risk, as you can see in the following graphics from the most recent investor presentation and 10Q (p 135).

Anticipated Fed cuts could pressure lending spreads and erode net investment income as BDCs refinance (likely at lower yields). You can see the percent floating versus fixed rates for most BDCs in our earlier table.

Credit defaults pose another threat. And while Trinity's non-accruals are low (as described earlier), broader private credit turmoil—from bankruptcies to tariff-impacted sectors—could hit its venture-backed holdings, especially with Trinity’s high payout ratio (i.e. limited buffer).

Lower 1st Lien Loans pose a risk. As you can see in the earlier BDC comparison table, Trinity sits at 76.6% on this metric, whereas other BDCs are considerably higher. First lien loans are considered safer (higher in the capital structure) and have lower default rates (as compared to secondary and tertiary liens and/or non-recourse loans).

Concentrated Growth Sector Exposure: With its concentration in growth and technology sectors, Trinity has been benefiting from the same “Growth/AI Bubble” that has many investors concerned. Even though Trinity is largely a debt investor, the BDC will still face pressures if/when the market cycle turns against growth and technology. For example, when the “Covid Bubble” burst in late 2021/2022, Trinity sold of hard as you can see in the following chart.

However and arguably, if the Fed plays its monetary policy cards right (with rate cuts), Trinity may not suffer the same fate (lower rates are good all sectors of the economy, especially growth and technology).

The Bottom Line

Whereas a lot of other BDCs focus on “old” economic sectors, Trinity’s niche focus on growthier and technology sectors gives its investors differentiated big-yield exposure. Not only is the outsized dividend yield attractive, but it’s generated from “newer” parts of the economy—arguably providing significantly more long-term growth potential.

However, the differentiated high income also comes with risks, including potential pain when the current growth-sector rally sells off (potentially significantly harder than more-value-focused BDCs) as we saw for Trinity when the covid-growth-bubble burst in 2021-2022 (see Trinity’s share price performance above).

Overall, I view Trinity as an attractive big-yield BDC that is absolutely worth considering. I own it (in my prudently diversified, long-term, income-focused portfolio).
